sourceFiles.name: .fixed-models/thermal-system__review-2026-03-26T09-10-14-037Z__selected-1-issues__2026-03-26T09-11-07-061Z.sysml

results.name: 04_redundancy
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A thermal control/protection system intended to monitor temperature and trigger protective or cooling responses.
- Operational domain: Likely industrial, embedded, or equipment thermal management.
- Model scope: Very small fragment containing two requirements and one part definition with temperature-related attributes.
- Known scope limitations: No behavioural model, no state machines, no packages beyond the root package, no verification cases, no interface/connectivity model, no decomposition hierarchy beyond a single part definition. Checks that require such artefacts are assessed as not evidenced in this fragment and skipped/passed with rationale rather than treated as defects.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Duplicate Requirements  -  The two requirements have different texts and different trigger temperatures ("80 degrees Celsius" vs "85 degrees Celsius"), so no requirement duplication is present in this fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Duplicate Block or Part Definitions  -  Only one `part def` (`CoolingSystem`) is present, with no parallel `item def` or alternate definition of the same entity to compare against.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Redundant Relationships  -  The fragment contains no `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, or specialization relationships, so no redundant relationship duplication is present.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Redundant Behaviour Models  -  No state machines, actions, sequences, or message exchange models are included in this fragment, so behavioural duplication cannot be observed here.

CHECK PASSED: 5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints  -  The attributes on `CoolingSystem` are distinct (`temperatureC`, `emergencyShutdownThresholdC`, `coolingFanStartThresholdC`) and do not duplicate each other by name or value semantics within the model text.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Redundant Decomposition  -  There is no decomposition hierarchy, intermediate pass-through block, empty package structure, or action decomposition in this small model fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Unintentional vs. Intentional Redundancy  -  No duplicated structural or requirements content was found, so there is no redundancy needing justification as intentional design redundancy.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-001]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem
Check:          8. States that are never activated
Finding:        The requirement `CoolingFanActivation` appears redundant relative to the emergency shutdown condition because the model states shutdown at 80°C while the fan activation requirement is triggered at 85°C. If shutdown precludes further operation, the fan activation condition would never be reached. This is a redundant/unreachable operational condition rather than duplicated text.
Evidence:       Requirement text 1: `The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius.` Requirement text 2: `The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius.` Related attribute in design: `attribute emergencyShutdownThresholdC : Real = 80.0;` while `attribute coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 75.0;` shows inconsistency between requirement and design threshold for fan start.
Score:          0.6  -  Moderate defect; an apparently unreachable requirement condition can mislead behaviour analysis, though the fragment lacks explicit behaviour proving shutdown disables fan operation.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  The finding depends on inferred system behaviour that emergency shutdown prevents later fan activation; the model provides requirements and thresholds but no explicit state machine or shutdown semantics.
Risk:           This can create dead or misleading requirements, inconsistent verification planning, and confusion over whether the fan is intended as a pre-shutdown mitigation or an unavailable post-shutdown response. Analysts may verify or implement the wrong threshold logic.
Recommendation: Clarify intent and remove the redundancy by either: (1) lowering the fan activation requirement below the shutdown threshold and aligning it with `coolingFanStartThresholdC = 75.0`; or (2) keeping the 85°C trigger only if shutdown does not inhibit fan operation, in which case add explicit behavioural semantics documenting that exception. If intentional, annotate the rationale.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| REDUNDANCY-001 | 0.6 | MEDIUM | Cooling fan activation at 85°C appears unreachable/redundant if emergency shutdown occurs at 80°C. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.6
Model Status: Significant issues - not suitable for baseline or verification activities
